content.text: Hypercare Escalation SLAs and War Room Protocols

During the hypercare period, incidents are more likely, more visible, and more urgent than during steady-state operations. Escalation SLAs define the maximum time allowed at each step before the next escalation tier is activated. War room protocols define how the team communicates, makes decisions, and closes incidents when multiple engineers are involved. Together they prevent the two most common hypercare incident failures: slow escalation and coordination overhead.

Escalation SLAs only work if every person in the chain knows them before the incident starts. Post the SLA table in the #hypercare channel and in the PagerDuty incident escalation policy before launch day.

This sub-subtopic covers P0/P1/P2 escalation SLA tables, war room protocol for P0 incidents, communication cadences, the Incident Commander role during hypercare, SLA compliance tracking, and the post-incident review trigger criteria.

attrs.alt: Hypercare escalation SLA timeline: P0 (5-min ACK → rollback by 30 min), P1 (15-min ACK → resolved by 4h), P2 (60-min ACK → resolved by 24h)

content.text: Auto-escalate to SRE lead; page feature engineer directly
EM opens war room; pages all required roles
IC or EM posts update if Comms Lead unavailable
Escalate to VP Engineering; prepare customer communication
Customer status page resolved or updated with ETA
Post extended impact notice; extend war room
Escalate to feature engineer if unresolved
Resolved or accepted workaround with ETA
Escalate to EM; consider P0 upgrade if impact widens
Resolved or known issue with ETA
Escalate to EM if no path to resolution

A war room is a focused communication space where all responders coordinate during a P0 incident. Undisciplined war rooms — where multiple people talk over each other, give conflicting instructions, or discuss root cause while the rollback is pending — double the time to recovery.
Single decision-maker. Calls rollback, calls all-clear, manages the room. Does NOT debug.
SRE lead or EM during hypercare
Owns diagnosis and execution. Reports findings to IC. Calls for help when needed.
Writes and posts all external updates. Does not coordinate technical response.
Customer success or designated comms person
Records timeline in incident record (alert fire time, actions taken, outcomes). Does not contribute to diagnosis.
Silent unless the IC asks a direct question. Additional engineers stay off mic unless contributing.
The IC does not debug. If the IC is also diagnosing, nobody is managing the room. These are two separate cognitive tasks that cannot share attention during a high-pressure incident.
One channel of record: all incident coordination in a single Slack thread; no DMs about the incident
Technical Lead speaks first in each update: status, hypothesis, next action, ETA
IC acknowledges and makes decisions; no discussion unless IC asks for input
No 'I think' without data: every claim is backed by a metric, log line, or command output
Silence is not acknowledgment: if IC calls your name and you don't respond in 30 seconds, you are assumed unavailable
Rollback is the IC's call, not a committee vote; Technical Lead executes, does not debate
P0 War Room Slack Message Templates

<!-- Template 1: War room open announcement -->
:rotating_light: *P0 Incident — War Room Open*
**Feature:** Claude API v2
**Alert:** RolloutLatencyP0 fired at 14:32 UTC
**IC:** @carol (SRE lead)
**Tech Lead:** @alice (feature engineer)
**Comms:** @bob
**Video bridge:** [link]
**Incident record:** [link]
All responders join bridge now. Observers mute.

---

<!-- Template 2: 20-minute status page update (during incident) -->
:warning: *Investigating — Claude API Latency*
We are investigating elevated response latency affecting a subset of Claude API requests.
Some requests may experience response times 30–60% above normal.
Next update in 15 minutes.
[status.anthropic.com](link)

---

<!-- Template 3: All-clear -->
:white_check_mark: *Resolved — Claude API Latency*
The elevated latency issue has been resolved.
Duration: 38 minutes
Impact: ~2% of requests affected
Root cause: identified, fix in progress
Full post-mortem within 5 business days.

During a P0 incident, external communication follows a fixed cadence regardless of whether there are new findings. A communication gap — no update for more than 15 minutes — is perceived by customers as disorganization and increases inbound support volume.
Status page: Update with impact scope if known
Internal: slack #launches update from IC; external: status page update
Status page: 'We are continuing to investigate. Next update in 15 min.'
Status page: 'Issue has been mitigated. Monitoring to confirm full recovery.'
Status page: Resolved. Internal: war room closed.
SLA compliance is tracked per incident and reviewed in the weekly hypercare standup. Patterns of missed SLAs indicate either understaffing, unclear escalation paths, or alert routing gaps.

# sla_tracker.py — record and evaluate SLA compliance for hypercare incidents
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Optional
import json

@dataclass
class IncidentSLARecord:
    incident_id: str
    severity: str
    alert_fired_at: datetime
    acknowledged_at: Optional[datetime] = None
    escalated_at: Optional[datetime] = None
    war_room_opened_at: Optional[datetime] = None
    mitigated_at: Optional[datetime] = None
    resolved_at: Optional[datetime] = None

    def sla_compliance(self) -> dict:
        slas = {
            "P0": {"acknowledge": 5, "escalate": 10, "war_room": 15, "mitigate": 30},
            "P1": {"acknowledge": 15, "escalate": 30, "resolve": 240},
            "P2": {"acknowledge": 60, "resolve": 1440},
        }
        thresholds = slas.get(self.severity, {})
        results = {}

        def minutes_elapsed(ts: Optional[datetime]) -> Optional[float]:
            if ts is None:
                return None
            return (ts - self.alert_fired_at).total_seconds() / 60

        if "acknowledge" in thresholds:
            elapsed = minutes_elapsed(self.acknowledged_at)
            results["acknowledge"] = {
                "elapsed_min": elapsed,
                "sla_min": thresholds["acknowledge"],
                "met": elapsed is not None and elapsed <= thresholds["acknowledge"],
            }
        if "mitigate" in thresholds:
            elapsed = minutes_elapsed(self.mitigated_at)
            results["mitigate"] = {
                "elapsed_min": elapsed,
                "sla_min": thresholds["mitigate"],
                "met": elapsed is not None and elapsed <= thresholds["mitigate"],
            }
        return results

# Example
record = IncidentSLARecord(
    incident_id="INC-20240415-001",
    severity="P0",
    alert_fired_at=datetime(2024, 4, 15, 14, 32),
    acknowledged_at=datetime(2024, 4, 15, 14, 34),   # 2 min — PASS
    mitigated_at=datetime(2024, 4, 15, 15, 10),       # 38 min — FAIL (> 30)
)
print(json.dumps(record.sla_compliance(), indent=2))
SLA Compliance Report — Weekly Hypercare Review
1 (SRE acked late due to PD gap)

* Week 1 P0 mitigate miss: one incident took 38 min to mitigate (target 30 min). Root cause: rollback command required IAM role not pre-granted to on-call SRE. Resolved in Week 2 by updating IAM policy.
Not every incident requires a full post-mortem. During hypercare, the trigger thresholds are lower than in steady-state because incidents are expected to surface systemic gaps.
Full blameless post-mortem with timeline and contributing factors
Abbreviated review focusing on SLA miss root cause
Full review; first occurrence missed systemic fix
Brief root cause note; share in hypercare standup
P1 resolved within SLA, no repeat
Notes in incident record; shared in weekly review

Hypercare SLAs are meaningless unless the alert-to-human path is configured correctly. A misconfigured escalation policy is the single most common cause of an SLA miss in the first week after launch. PagerDuty escalation policies must be reviewed and tested before go-live.

A correct hypercare escalation policy has three layers: an immediate on-call layer, a manager escalation layer (fires if the on-call does not acknowledge), and a war room trigger layer (fires automatically at the P0 SLA boundary).

# PagerDuty escalation policy — hypercare tier
name: claude-hypercare-p0
escalation_rules:
  - escalation_delay_in_minutes: 5        # if no ACK in 5 min → escalate
    targets:
      - type: user
        id: sre-on-call-primary
      - type: schedule
        id: feature-eng-primary-schedule

  - escalation_delay_in_minutes: 10       # if still no ACK → page SRE manager
    targets:
      - type: user
        id: sre-manager
      - type: user
        id: feature-eng-lead

  - escalation_delay_in_minutes: 15       # 15 min without ACK → wake everyone
    targets:
      - type: schedule
        id: all-hands-hypercare-bridge
        # This schedule has every hypercare team member as a target.
        # At 15 min without acknowledgment the full war room must be assumed.

Each alert must route to the correct escalation policy based on severity. Routing is defined by matching on alert labels. During hypercare, the routing table should be simpler than steady-state — err toward over-alerting.
Fast burn: 1h budget gone in ~4 min
Config mismatch between canary and stable

Test every alert route in staging before go-live. The most reliable test is to manually fire a synthetic alert with each label and confirm the correct on-call engineer receives a page within 60 seconds.
On-Call Handoff During an Active Incident

A war room may span a shift handoff. When an incident begins at 23:00 UTC and a resolution is not reached before the 06:00 UTC shift change, the incoming on-call engineer must receive a complete handoff before the outgoing IC leaves the bridge. An incomplete handoff is a primary cause of regression — the incoming IC may repeat diagnosis already done or undo progress.
Outgoing IC calls the handoff 30 minutes before shift end — do not wait until the last minute.
Outgoing IC delivers verbal SBAR: Situation (what is broken), Background (what was tried), Assessment (current hypothesis and evidence), Recommendation (next action and who owns it).
Incoming IC acknowledges each point verbally and names any gaps before accepting the handoff.
Scribe documents the handoff time and the names of outgoing and incoming ICs in the incident record.
Outgoing IC remains available on Slack for 30 minutes post-handoff to answer questions — not on the bridge.
Incident record URL shared in war room channel
All open mitigation actions and their owners listed
Rollback readiness state confirmed (ready / blocked / executed)
Next scheduled status page update time noted
Incoming IC has prod access verified

# handoff_record.py — structured handoff record written to incident doc
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class IncidentHandoff:
    incident_id: str
    outgoing_ic: str
    incoming_ic: str
    handoff_time: datetime
    situation: str              # One sentence: what is broken and who is affected
    background: str             # What has been tried; what was ruled out
    current_hypothesis: str     # Best current explanation for root cause
    next_action: str            # Specific next step and who owns it
    rollback_state: str         # 'ready' | 'executing' | 'completed' | 'blocked:<reason>'
    comms_lead: Optional[str] = None
    notes: str = ""

# Example handoff record
handoff = IncidentHandoff(
    incident_id="INC-20240416-003",
    outgoing_ic="alice@anthropic.com",
    incoming_ic="bob@anthropic.com",
    handoff_time=datetime(2024, 4, 16, 6, 0),
    situation="Claude API model-serving p99 latency is 2.4× above baseline; ~5% of requests affected",
    background="Ruled out: DB connection pool exhaustion, network congestion, upstream LLM provider. "
               "Canary at 15%; rolled back to 5% at T+40 min — did not resolve. "
               "Suspect new tokenizer version introduced memory allocation regression.",
    current_hypothesis="Tokenizer v2.1.0 allocates ~40% more peak memory per request; "
                       "model server GC is the bottleneck under load.",
    next_action="alice to send tokenizer memory profile to @charlie before signing off. "
                "bob to initiate rollback to tokenizer v2.0.3 if profile confirms hypothesis.",
    rollback_state="ready",
    comms_lead="carol@anthropic.com",
    notes="Status page update due at T+75 min (06:15 UTC). Carol is staying on."
)
The following behaviors are observed in poorly-run war rooms and reliably extend incident duration. They are documented here so teams can recognize and correct them in real time.
IC is running commands and reading logs instead of directing
IC assigns debugging tasks; does not execute them personally
Team exploring symptoms without a current best hypothesis
IC demands a hypothesis before allowing continued investigation; 'we don't know yet' is a valid hypothesis
Two engineers attempting different rollbacks simultaneously
IC chooses one rollback path; all others wait for the result
Comms Lead posts 'still investigating' on the 15-minute cadence even with no new findings
Statements like 'who deployed this?' or 'this should never have been approved'
All post-attribution happens in the post-mortem; in the war room only 'what is the current state?' is relevant
Engineers opening separate Slack threads, private calls, or side incidents
All communication goes through one war room channel and one bridge; IC calls anyone who goes dark

Hypercare escalation SLAs and war room protocols prevent the two most common incident failures during post-launch periods: slow escalation and uncoordinated response. P0 incidents require acknowledgment within 5 minutes, war room open within 15 minutes, and mitigation within 30 minutes. P1 incidents require acknowledgment within 15 minutes and resolution within 4 hours. The Incident Commander owns the room and makes decisions; the Technical Lead executes; the Communications Lead handles all external updates on a 15-minute cadence. SLA compliance is tracked weekly and reviewed in the hypercare standup — missed SLAs are treated as systemic gaps, not individual failures, and each miss generates a root cause investigation before the next week.
P0 SLAs: ACK 5 min → escalate to eng 10 min → war room open 15 min → mitigate 30 min → all-clear 4h
P1 SLAs: ACK 15 min → diagnose 30 min → customer update 2h → resolved 4h
War room roles: IC (decisions), Tech Lead (execution), Comms Lead (external updates), Scribe (timeline)
Status page updates every 15 minutes during P0 — silence reads as disorganization to customers
SLA compliance tracked weekly; any miss requires root cause note before next week's standup
Post-mortem triggered by: every P0, any SLA-missing P1, any repeated P1 within 7 days
